question: What is a ray in the context of light?
answer: a straight line showing the path that light travels along
accept: a straight line showing the direction light travels
a line showing the path of light
misconceptions: a curved line light follows
a beam that bends around objects
the source of the light

question: Why do we draw light as straight arrows?
answer: because light always travels in straight lines
an arrow shows the direction the light is travelling
accept: light goes in straight lines
straight lines show its path and direction
misconceptions: light bends around objects
light travels in waves so we draw curves
arrows show how fast light is going

question: What type of wave is light?
answer: transverse
accept: a transverse wave
misconceptions: longitudinal
a sound wave
a mechanical wave

question: Explain why light can travel through a vacuum but sound cannot.
answer: sound needs particles to travel
a vacuum has no particles, but light does not need particles to travel
accept: light does not need particles, sound does
there are no particles in a vacuum so sound has nothing to travel through but light does not need them
misconceptions: light needs particles just like sound
a vacuum is full of air
sound is faster so it can cross a vacuum

question: What is a vacuum?
answer: a space with no particles at all
accept: a region containing no particles
empty space with no particles, such as outer space
misconceptions: a space full of air
a space with only a few particles
a space full of light

question: Which statement about how fast light travels in a vacuum is correct?
answer: nearly 300 million metres per second
options: it cannot travel in a vacuum
distractors: this is far too slow; it confuses the magnitude of the value
sound is much slower than light and cannot even cross a vacuum
light travels fastest in a vacuum, not zero speed
misconceptions: light travels at the same speed as sound
light cannot travel through a vacuum

question: In which medium does light travel fastest, and why?
answer: light travels fastest in a vacuum
because there are no particles at all to interact with
accept: fastest in a vacuum as there are no particles
a vacuum, because the fewer particles there are the faster light goes
misconceptions: fastest in a solid
fastest where there are most particles
light needs particles so it is fastest in a solid

question: Light travels faster in gases than in solids. Explain why.
answer: light interacts with the particles in a medium
there are fewer particles in a gas than a solid, so light travels faster
accept: fewer particles in a gas means light is slowed less
gases have fewer particles to interact with so light is faster
misconceptions: light needs particles to travel, so more particles means faster
solids let light through fastest
light is faster in solids because they are denser

question: What is a shadow?
answer: the absence of light
accept: a dark patch where light has been blocked
an area light cannot reach because an object blocks it
misconceptions: a dark light given off by an object
a reflection of an object
something an object makes by absorbing all light

question: Why does an object block light and create a shadow?
answer: light cannot bend around objects
so where the object blocks the light there is a dark patch with no light
accept: light travels in straight lines and cannot go round the object
the object stops the light, leaving an area of no light behind it
misconceptions: light bends around the object
the object gives out darkness
the shadow is light reflected backwards

question: A torch lights up a whole room, not just straight ahead. How do we represent this with a ray model?
answer: as many individual straight lines spreading out in different directions
options: as a single curved line that fills the room
as many individual straight lines spreading out in different directions
as one straight line going forwards only
as wavy lines that bend around furniture
distractors: light does not curve; rays are always drawn straight
light spreads out, so a single forward line is not enough
light cannot bend around objects, so wavy bending lines are wrong
misconceptions: light bends to fill a room
light only goes straight ahead from a torch

question: What is oscillating in a light wave?
answer: the electromagnetic field
accept: electric and magnetic fields
the electric and magnetic field
misconceptions: particles
air particles
the medium it travels through

question: How does changing the wavelength of light change what we see, and which way makes it bluer?
answer: changing the wavelength changes the colour
a shorter wavelength makes the light bluer
accept: wavelength changes colour; shorter wavelength is bluer
a longer wavelength is redder and a shorter one is bluer
misconceptions: wavelength changes the brightness
a longer wavelength makes light bluer
changing wavelength makes light dimmer

question: Roughly how fast does light travel in a vacuum, in millions of metres per second?
answer: 300 million m/s
working: Light travels at nearly 300 million metres per second in a vacuum.

question: State the three things that can happen to light when it hits a new material.
answer: it can be transmitted
it can be absorbed
it can be reflected
accept: transmitted, absorbed or reflected
reflected, transmitted or absorbed
misconceptions: it disappears
it is created
it always passes straight through

question: What is a spectrum?
answer: the smooth, continuous range of colours that white light splits into
accept: the range of colours white light separates into
a smooth continuous range of colours
misconceptions: a single pure colour
a mixture that stays white
just red and blue light

question: List, in order, the colours of the spectrum that white light splits into.
answer: red, orange, yellow, green, blue, indigo, violet
accept: red orange yellow green blue indigo violet
misconceptions: red, yellow and blue only
red, green and blue only
just the colours of paint

question: Explain how a prism splits white light into a spectrum.
answer: the white light refracts as it enters and leaves the prism
white light is made up of all the colours
each colour refracts a different amount, so the colours spread out
accept: it refracts at each surface and each colour bends a different amount
white light contains all the colours and they refract by different amounts so they separate
misconceptions: the prism adds colour to the light
white light is a single pure colour
the prism reflects the colours apart

question: In a spectrum produced by a prism, which colour is refracted the least?
answer: red
distractors: violet refracts the most, not the least
green is in the middle of the spectrum, not refracted least
white light is the mixture that gets split, not a colour in the spectrum
misconceptions: violet bends the least
white is one of the spectrum colours

question: What two things are needed for a rainbow to form, and what process causes it?
answer: white light from the Sun and rain (water droplets)
refraction of the white light as it passes through the droplets
accept: sunlight and raindrops; refraction splits the light
light from the Sun and water droplets, which refract the light into colours
misconceptions: a prism in the sky
reflection off clouds
the rain is coloured

question: Why does a blue object appear blue in white light?
answer: it reflects blue light into our eyes
it absorbs the red and green light
accept: it reflects blue and absorbs the other colours
blue is reflected and the rest is absorbed
misconceptions: it gives out its own blue light
it absorbs blue and reflects the rest
the object is made of blue light

question: Why does a black object appear black?
answer: it absorbs all colours of light and reflects none
options: it gives out its own black light
it absorbs all colours of light and reflects none
it reflects all colours of light
it transmits all the light through it
distractors: black is the absence of light, not a light that is given out
reflecting all colours would make an object look white
an opaque object does not transmit light through it
misconceptions: a black object gives out black light
black objects reflect every colour

question: Why does a white object appear white in white light?
answer: it reflects all the colours of light
red, green and blue light are all reflected into our eyes
accept: it reflects every colour and absorbs none
it reflects red, green and blue so we see white
misconceptions: it absorbs all the colours
white light is a single colour it reflects
it gives out its own white light

question: Our eyes detect only red, green and blue light. Why do we still see white light as white?
answer: our brain combines the red, green and blue signals together
when red, green and blue light are combined our eyes see this as white
accept: the brain mixes the red, green and blue signals into white
combining red, green and blue light looks white to us
misconceptions: white is a single pure colour
our eyes detect white directly
white light has no colours in it

question: What does a coloured filter do to light?
answer: it only lets some colours of light through (transmits some and blocks others)
accept: it transmits some colours and not others
it lets certain colours pass and stops the rest
misconceptions: it adds colour to the light
it reflects all the light
it lets all colours through equally

question: White light is shone through a red filter. What colour passes through, and what happens to the other colours?
answer: only red light is transmitted through the filter
the green and blue light are not transmitted (they are blocked)
accept: red passes through; green and blue are stopped
only red gets through and the rest is blocked
misconceptions: all colours pass through
the filter turns the light red by adding colour
red is blocked and the rest passes

question: White light passes through a red filter and then hits a blue object. What colour does the object appear?
answer: black
distractors: the object is blue but there is no blue light reaching it to reflect
a blue object cannot reflect red light, so it does not appear red
only one colour of light is present, so it cannot appear white
misconceptions: the object keeps its own colour whatever light hits it
a blue object reflects red light

question: White light passes through a green filter and hits a white object. What colour does the object appear?
answer: green
distractors: only green light reaches the object, so it cannot appear white
there is no red light present after the green filter
a white object reflects whatever light hits it, so it does not appear black
misconceptions: a white object always appears white
a white object absorbs the green light

question: How many different colours are usually named in the visible spectrum that white light splits into (red, orange, yellow, green, blue, indigo, violet)?
working: The spectrum is red, orange, yellow, green, blue, indigo, violet = 7 colours.

question: Our eyes use three types of colour-detecting cell, for red, green and blue light. How many of these colours must be combined to make our eyes see white?
working: Red + green + blue light combined are seen as white, so 3 colours are needed.

question: What does it mean to say that light is transmitted through a material?
answer: the light (energy) passes through to the other side of the material
accept: it travels through and comes out the other side
misconceptions: the light is absorbed by the material
the light bounces back
the material gives out its own light

question: What does it mean to say that light is absorbed by a material?
answer: the light is blocked / taken in by the material
so no wave makes it out of the other side
accept: the energy is taken in and does not pass through
misconceptions: the light passes straight through
the light bounces back into the first medium
the material reflects the light

question: What does it mean to say that light is reflected by a material?
answer: the light bounces back into the medium it came from
accept: it bounces off the surface
misconceptions: the light passes through the material
the light is absorbed and disappears
the material bends the light

question: Which word describes a material that lets all light pass straight through it?
answer: Transparent
distractors: Translucent materials only let some light through, not all
Opaque materials let no light through at all
Reflective describes bouncing light back, not transmitting it
misconceptions: confusing transparent with translucent
thinking opaque means see-through

question: Which word describes a material that lets no light pass through it at all?
answer: Opaque
distractors: Transparent materials let all light through
Translucent materials let some light through
Refractive describes bending, not blocking, light
misconceptions: thinking opaque means partly see-through
confusing opaque with translucent

question: What is a translucent material? Explain what happens to light that hits it.
answer: a material that lets some light through
some light is absorbed but at least some is transmitted, so it looks dimmer
accept: lets part of the light pass so it appears dimmer
in between transparent and opaque
misconceptions: it lets all the light through
it lets no light through
it is the same as transparent

question: A mirror does not let any light pass through it. Explain why a mirror is described as opaque even though it is not dull or dark.
answer: opaque means it does not transmit any light through it
a mirror reflects the light instead of transmitting it, so it is still opaque
accept: an opaque object can reflect light, it just cannot transmit any
misconceptions: opaque objects must absorb all the light
a mirror is transparent because it is shiny
only dark objects are opaque

question: Why does an object seen through a translucent material look dimmer than through a transparent one?
answer: the translucent material absorbs some of the light
so less energy (light) is transferred through than with a transparent material
accept: only some light gets through so the amplitude is lower
misconceptions: the translucent material adds darkness
translucent materials reflect all the light
the light changes colour

question: State what is meant by refraction.
answer: refraction is when light changes direction (bends) as it passes from one medium to another
accept: the bending of light as it changes medium
misconceptions: refraction is when light bounces off a surface
refraction is when light is absorbed
refraction is when light splits into colours

question: Explain why light refracts (bends) when it passes from air into glass at an angle.
answer: light changes speed when it enters a new medium
because it enters at an angle, one side slows before the other, so its direction changes
accept: it slows down in the glass and changes direction because it hits the boundary at an angle
misconceptions: light bends because the glass is heavier
light always bends when it hits glass even at 90 degrees
the glass pushes the light sideways

question: For light to refract at the boundary between two materials, which condition must be met?
answer: The light must hit the boundary at an angle other than 90 degrees
options: The light must hit the boundary at an angle other than 90 degrees
The light must hit the boundary exactly along the normal
The two materials must be the same
The light must be reflected first
distractors: Light hitting along the normal (at 90 degrees) does not change direction
If the materials are the same there is no change of medium and no refraction
Reflection is a separate interaction and is not required for refraction
misconceptions: thinking light refracts even when it enters at 90 degrees
thinking refraction happens without a change of medium

question: A ray of light enters a glass block exactly at 90 degrees to the surface (along the normal). Does it change direction? Explain your answer.
answer: no, it does not change direction
because it is travelling along the normal, so it does not refract even though it slows down
accept: it carries straight on because it hits at 90 degrees
misconceptions: it always bends when entering glass
it bends towards the normal
it bends away from the normal

question: What happens to the speed of light as it travels from air into a glass block?
answer: it slows down
accept: light travels slower in glass than in air
misconceptions: it speeds up
it stays the same
it stops

question: What happens to the speed of light as it leaves a glass block and travels back out into the air?
answer: it speeds up
accept: light travels faster in air than in glass
misconceptions: it slows down
it stays the same
it stops

question: As light travels from air into glass at an angle, in which direction does it bend relative to the normal?
answer: it bends towards the normal
accept: towards the normal line
misconceptions: it bends away from the normal
it does not bend
it bends along the surface

question: As light travels from glass back out into air at an angle, in which direction does it bend relative to the normal?
answer: it bends away from the normal
accept: away from the normal line
misconceptions: it bends towards the normal
it does not bend
it travels back the way it came

question: What is the normal line in a ray diagram?
answer: an imaginary line drawn at 90 degrees to the surface (boundary)
accept: a line at right angles to the surface
misconceptions: a line drawn along the surface
the path the light travels
the reflected ray

question: Why do we always measure the angles of a refracting or reflecting ray from the normal line?
answer: The normal is a fixed reference at 90 degrees to the surface so the angle is consistent
options: The normal is a fixed reference at 90 degrees to the surface so the angle is consistent
Because the normal is the path the light travels
Because the angle to the surface is always 90 degrees
Because the normal is the reflected ray
distractors: The normal is an imaginary reference line, not the light path
The angle to the surface varies; that is why we use the normal instead
The normal is a reference line, not the reflected ray
misconceptions: measuring the angle from the surface instead of the normal
thinking the normal is the light ray

question: When light passes through a glass block at an angle, some light is seen reflecting at the surfaces as well as passing through. Explain why.
answer: each time light hits a change in medium, some of the light is reflected
this happens at the air-to-glass boundary and again at the glass-to-air boundary
accept: some light always reflects at every boundary between two mediums
misconceptions: all the light is transmitted with none reflected
reflection only happens at mirrors
the glass gives out its own light

question: In which state of matter does light travel slowest?
answer: Solids
distractors: Light travels faster in liquids than solids because there are fewer particles
Light travels even faster in gases than liquids
Light travels fastest of all in a vacuum, not slowest
misconceptions: thinking light is slowest in a vacuum
thinking light is slowest in gases

question: Explain why light travels faster in a gas than in a solid, even though light does not need particles to travel.
answer: light does interact with particles as it passes through a medium
there are fewer particles in a gas than in a solid, so light can travel faster
accept: fewer particles means less interaction so light goes faster
misconceptions: light needs particles to travel like sound does
more particles make light faster
light travels at the same speed everywhere

question: Using ideas about particles, explain why light travels faster in air than in water.
answer: air has fewer particles than water (air is less dense)
fewer particles means light interacts less and so travels faster
accept: water is denser with more particles so light slows down in it
misconceptions: light needs the particles to move so more particles is faster
water has no particles
the colour of the water changes the speed

question: A table shows that as the density of a medium increases, the speed of light in it decreases. Write the relationship between density and the speed of light as a trend.
answer: as the density of the medium decreases, the speed of light in the medium increases
accept: the lower the density, the faster light travels
higher density means slower light
misconceptions: as density increases, the speed of light increases
density does not affect the speed of light
light has the same speed in every medium

question: A ray of light passes from water into a pocket of air inside the water, hitting the boundary at an angle. State how its speed changes and which way it bends.
answer: it speeds up as it enters the air (fewer particles / less dense)
it bends away from the normal
accept: faster in air and bends away from the normal
misconceptions: it slows down going into the air
it bends towards the normal
it does not change direction

question: Light travels at about 300 million metres per second (3 x 10^8 m/s) in a vacuum. How far does it travel in 3 seconds?
working: distance = speed x time = 300000000 x 3 = 900000000 m
accept: 900 million metres
misconceptions: 100000000 m (dividing instead of multiplying)
300000000 m (forgetting the 3 seconds)

question: A window lets you see clearly through it, but it also produces a faint reflection of your face. Which two interactions of light with the glass does this show?
answer: Transmission and reflection
distractors: If the glass mainly absorbed light you could not see clearly through it
Absorption would block the view rather than let you see through
Refraction bends the light but the question is about seeing through and the reflection
misconceptions: thinking clear glass only transmits and never reflects
thinking a faint reflection means the glass is opaque

question: Compare what happens to light passing through a transparent material with light passing through an opaque material.
answer: all the light is transmitted through a transparent material
no light is transmitted through an opaque material (it is absorbed or reflected)
accept: transparent lets all light through; opaque lets none through
misconceptions: both let some light through
opaque lets a little light through
transparent absorbs the light

question: A ray of light shines from air, through a rectangular glass block, and out into the air again. Describe how its speed and direction change as it passes through the block.
answer: entering the glass it slows down and bends towards the normal
while inside the glass it travels in a straight line
leaving the glass it speeds up and bends away from the normal
accept: slows and turns towards normal on entry, speeds up and turns away from normal on exit
misconceptions: light speeds up entering the glass
light bends away from the normal on entry
light keeps changing direction inside the block

question: Light travels at about 300 million metres per second (3 x 10^8 m/s) in a vacuum. How far does it travel in 2 seconds?
working: distance = speed x time = 300000000 x 2 = 600000000 m
accept: 600 million metres
misconceptions: 150000000 m (dividing instead of multiplying)
300000000 m (forgetting the 2 seconds)

subtopic: Mirrors, pinhole cameras and the eye
question: What is the name of the ray of light that travels towards a mirror?
answer: the incident ray
accept: incident ray
the ray coming in
misconceptions: the reflected ray
the normal
the refracted ray

subtopic: Mirrors, pinhole cameras and the eye
question: What is the name of the imaginary line drawn at 90 degrees to a mirror's surface, used to measure angles?
answer: the normal
or normal line
accept: normal
normal line
misconceptions: the incident ray
the boundary line
the reflected ray

subtopic: Mirrors, pinhole cameras and the eye
question: When measuring the angle of incidence at a mirror, where must the angle be measured from?
answer: from the normal line
options: from the surface of the mirror
from the incident ray to the reflected ray
from the edge of the paper
distractors: a common error is to measure from the mirror surface, which gives a wrong value such as 67 degrees
angles are not measured between the two rays directly
the edge of the paper is irrelevant to the angle
misconceptions: measuring from the mirror surface instead of the normal
measuring between the incident and reflected rays

subtopic: Mirrors, pinhole cameras and the eye
question: A student measures the angle of incidence at a mirror as 67 degrees, but the correct answer is much smaller. Explain the mistake they have most likely made.
answer: they measured the angle from the surface of the mirror
instead of from the normal line
accept: they measured to the mirror line not the normal
they did not measure from the normal
misconceptions: the protractor was faulty
light does not travel in straight lines
the mirror was at the wrong angle

subtopic: Mirrors, pinhole cameras and the eye
question: Why is a mirror described as opaque even though it bounces light back?
answer: because it does not transmit (let light through) it
accept: no light passes through it
it reflects light rather than letting it through
misconceptions: it is transparent because you can see a reflection
it is translucent because some light passes through

subtopic: Mirrors, pinhole cameras and the eye
question: What happens to all the light that hits a mirror?
answer: all of it is reflected
accept: it all bounces off
it is all reflected back
misconceptions: some is transmitted through the mirror
most is absorbed by the mirror

subtopic: Mirrors, pinhole cameras and the eye
question: What piece of equipment is used to measure the angle of incidence and angle of reflection?
answer: a protractor
accept: protractor
misconceptions: a ruler
a ray box
a set square

subtopic: Mirrors, pinhole cameras and the eye
question: What does the word 'normal' mean in maths, as used when drawing the normal line?
answer: 90 degrees (a right angle)
accept: at right angles
perpendicular
misconceptions: ordinary or usual
horizontal
a straight line

subtopic: Mirrors, pinhole cameras and the eye
question: Why must we always draw light rays travelling to and from a mirror as straight arrows?
answer: because light always travels in straight lines
accept: light only travels in straight lines
light cannot bend on its own
misconceptions: because rays can curve around the mirror
because the mirror bends the light into a curve

subtopic: Mirrors, pinhole cameras and the eye
question: A ray of light hits a mirror with an angle of incidence of 40 degrees. The angle of reflection equals the angle of incidence. What is the angle of reflection?
answer: 40 degrees
working: angle of reflection = angle of incidence = 40 degrees
misconceptions: 50 degrees (treating it as the complement to 90)
80 degrees (doubling it)
0 degrees

subtopic: Mirrors, pinhole cameras and the eye
question: A ray of light strikes a mirror at 25 degrees measured from the mirror's surface. What is the angle of incidence, measured correctly from the normal?
answer: 65 degrees
misconceptions: 25 degrees (measuring from the surface, not the normal)
115 degrees
50 degrees

subtopic: Mirrors, pinhole cameras and the eye
question: What is a lens?
answer: something we use to manipulate (change) the direction that light travels
accept: a piece of glass or plastic that bends light
an object that changes the direction of light
misconceptions: a mirror that reflects light
a hole that lets light in

subtopic: Mirrors, pinhole cameras and the eye
question: Why does light change direction as it passes through a lens?
answer: because it refracts at the curved boundary
accept: it refracts at the surface
the light bends as it changes medium
misconceptions: because the lens reflects the light
because the light is absorbed and re-emitted

subtopic: Mirrors, pinhole cameras and the eye
question: What shape do the lenses studied in this topic have?
answer: a curved shape
distractors: flat rectangular blocks are what was studied for refraction, not lenses
a triangular shape describes a prism, not a lens
lenses are not square
misconceptions: lenses are flat rectangular blocks
lenses are triangular like a prism

subtopic: Mirrors, pinhole cameras and the eye
question: What name is given to the effect of a lens forcing all the light rays to meet at a single point?
answer: focusing
accept: focus
focussing
misconceptions: reflecting
refracting
magnifying

subtopic: Mirrors, pinhole cameras and the eye
question: Explain why light hitting the very centre of a lens passes straight through without bending.
answer: it enters along the normal line of the boundary
light hitting along the normal (at 90 degrees) does not change direction
accept: it goes in at 90 degrees so it does not refract
it travels along the normal so it is not bent
misconceptions: the centre of the lens is a hole

subtopic: Mirrors, pinhole cameras and the eye
question: Explain why light hitting the edge of a lens is bent, while light hitting the centre is not.
answer: at the edge the light does not enter along the normal
because the normal is at 90 degrees to the curved surface so it is not horizontal there
so the light refracts and changes direction
accept: the normal is not horizontal at the edge so light is bent
light at the edge does not travel along the normal
misconceptions: the edge of the lens is thicker so it absorbs light
the edge reflects the light

subtopic: Mirrors, pinhole cameras and the eye
question: In a microscope or telescope, what is the main job of the lens?
answer: to magnify the image and make it clearer
options: to reflect light back to the user
to magnify the image and make it clearer
to split white light into colours
distractors: lenses refract light, they do not reflect it like a mirror
the lens does not work by absorbing light
splitting light into colours is what a prism does
misconceptions: a lens reflects light like a mirror
a lens splits light into a spectrum

subtopic: Mirrors, pinhole cameras and the eye
question: Which part of the eye is the coloured ring around the pupil?
answer: the iris
accept: iris
misconceptions: the pupil
the retina
the lens

subtopic: Mirrors, pinhole cameras and the eye
question: Which part of the eye is the hole that lets light in?
answer: the pupil
accept: pupil
misconceptions: the iris
the lens
the retina

subtopic: Mirrors, pinhole cameras and the eye
question: Which part of the eye focuses the light to a point at the back of the eye?
answer: the lens
distractors: the pupil is the hole that lets light in, it does not focus
the iris is the coloured part, it does not focus light
the optic nerve carries signals to the brain, it does not focus light
misconceptions: the pupil focuses the light
the retina focuses the light

subtopic: Mirrors, pinhole cameras and the eye
question: In which region at the back of the eye is light detected?
answer: the retina
accept: retina
misconceptions: the optic nerve
the iris
the cornea

subtopic: Mirrors, pinhole cameras and the eye
question: Describe what the retina does when a light wave hits it.
answer: it detects the light
it generates electrical signals
accept: it turns light into electrical signals
it produces electrical signals when light hits it
misconceptions: it focuses the light
it reflects the light back
it lets the light through to the brain

subtopic: Mirrors, pinhole cameras and the eye
question: What carries the electrical signals from the retina to the brain?
answer: the optic nerve
accept: optic nerve
misconceptions: the auditory nerve
the lens
the iris

subtopic: Mirrors, pinhole cameras and the eye
question: Describe the path light takes through the eye, in order, from entering to reaching the brain.
answer: light passes through the pupil
it meets the lens which focuses it
onto the retina at the back of the eye
and signals travel along the optic nerve to the brain
accept: pupil, lens, retina, optic nerve, brain
misconceptions: light enters through the iris
the retina focuses light before the lens
the eye sends light out to the object

subtopic: Mirrors, pinhole cameras and the eye
question: What is a pinhole camera?
answer: the simplest type of camera, a box with a tiny hole the size of a pin
accept: a box with a tiny pin-sized hole that forms an image
a simple camera with a tiny hole instead of a lens
misconceptions: a camera that uses a large lens
a camera with a big hole to let in lots of light

subtopic: Mirrors, pinhole cameras and the eye
question: Which way up is the image formed on the screen of a pinhole camera?
answer: upside down (inverted)
options: the same way up as the object
distractors: the rays cross at the pinhole so the image is not the right way up
the image is not rotated to its side, it is inverted top-to-bottom
the image is not the same orientation as the object because the rays cross
misconceptions: the image is the right way up
the image is the same way up as the object

subtopic: Mirrors, pinhole cameras and the eye
question: Explain why a pinhole camera can form a focused image even though it has no lens.
answer: the hole is so tiny that there is only one path light can take from each part of the object
so the light is naturally focused as it all meets at the single pinhole
accept: only one ray from each point passes through the tiny hole
the small hole means light is focused without a lens
misconceptions: the box bends the light like a lens
the hole magnifies the light
the screen focuses the light

subtopic: Mirrors, pinhole cameras and the eye
question: What happens to the image in a pinhole camera if the hole is made large instead of tiny?
answer: no clear image forms, just a bright spot
options: the image becomes sharper and brighter
no clear image forms, just a bright spot
the image turns the right way up
the image is magnified and clearer
distractors: a large hole does not sharpen the image, it ruins it
a large hole does not flip the image upright
a large hole does not magnify a clear image
misconceptions: a bigger hole gives a sharper image
a bigger hole makes the image brighter and clearer

subtopic: Mirrors, pinhole cameras and the eye
question: Explain why a large hole in a pinhole camera produces only a bright spot and no image.
answer: light can pass through the large hole at many different angles
so the rays from each part of the object overlap instead of forming a clear image
accept: many paths of light pass through so the image is blurred into a bright spot
there is more than one path for light from each point, so no image forms
misconceptions: the large hole lets in too much colour
the large hole bends the light the wrong way
a big hole reflects the light
